慎的混合：大家既想快點知道，也想知道得正確。]

components.english: For instance, someone may first hear about a major event from a friend’s post or a short video online, but then switch to a news channel or read a full article to understand what actually happened. Older people, in particular, may still prefer TV news or radio because it feels steadier and more familiar.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人可能先透過朋友的帖子，或者一段短片知道一件大事，但之後還是會去看新聞台，或者讀一篇完整報道，才真正了解事情的來龍去脈。特別是一些年長人士，仍然可能更喜歡電視新聞或電台，因為那種方式感覺較穩妥，也較熟悉。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, someone may first hear about a major
event from a friend’s post or a short video online, but then switch to a
news channel or read a full article to understand what actually
happened. Older people, in particular, may still prefer TV news or
radio because it feels steadier and more familiar.
[例如，一個人可能先透過朋友的帖子，或者一段短片知道一件大事，但之後還是會去看新
聞台，或者讀一篇完整報道，才真正了解事情的來龍去脈。特別是一些年長人士，仍然可能
更喜歡電視新聞或電台，因為那種方式感覺較穩妥，也較熟悉。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people in today’s society get their news from a mixture of digital and traditional sources, but digital platforms clearly lead the way. News now travels faster than ever, but that also means people have to be more careful about what they believe.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，今天的人是透過數碼和傳統渠道混合地接收新聞，只不過數碼平台明顯已經走在前面。如今新聞傳播得比以前快得多，但這也意味著，人們必須更加小心自己相信的是甚麼。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say people in today’s society get their
news from a mixture of digital and traditional sources, but digital
platforms clearly lead the way. News now travels faster than ever, but
that also means people have to be more careful about what they
believe.
[總結來說，我會說，今天的人是透過數碼和傳統渠道混合地接收新聞，只不過數碼平台明
顯已經走在前面。如今新聞傳播得比以前快得多，但這也意味著，人們必須更加小心自己相
信的是甚麼。]

question.english: How do you think people will get their news in the future?
question.chinese: 主題：你認為未來人們會怎樣獲取新聞？

source_lines.text: Topic: How do you think people will get their news in the future?
[主題：你認為未來人們會怎樣獲取新聞？]

components.english: I think people will get their news in faster, more personalised, and more mobile ways, mainly through their phones rather than through traditional routines like sitting down for the evening news.
components.chinese: 我認為，未來人們獲取新聞的方式會變得更快、更個人化，也更依賴流動裝置，主要會透過手機，而不是像以前那樣坐下來等晚間新聞。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think people will get their news in faster, more personalised,
and more mobile ways, mainly through their phones rather than
through traditional routines like sitting down for the evening news.
[我認為，未來人們獲取新聞的方式會變得更快、更個人化，也更依賴流動裝置，主要會透
過手機，而不是像以前那樣坐下來等晚間新聞。]

components.english: The reason is quite straightforward: the centre of gravity has already shifted. Current research points to falling engagement with traditional media and growing dependence on social media, video networks, search, and other digital pathways. So, in the future, news will probably come to people in shorter bursts, through feeds, alerts, clips, and links, rather than in one fixed package. In a way, news is becoming less like a newspaper on the doorstep and more like a stream that follows you around.
components.chinese: 原因其實很直接：重心本身已經開始轉移。現有研究指出，人們對傳統媒體的投入正在下降，而對社交媒體、影片平台、搜尋，以及其他數碼途徑的依賴則在增加。所以，未來的新聞很可能會以更短、更碎片化的形式出現，透過動態消息、通知、短片和連結不斷送到人們面前，而不是像以前那樣被打包成一份固定產品。某程度上，新聞已經愈來愈不像一份送到門口的報紙，反而更像一條一直跟着你流動的資訊河。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is quite straightforward: the centre of
gravity has already shifted. Current research points to falling
engagement with traditional media and growing dependence on social
media, video networks, search, and other digital pathways. So, in the
future, news will probably come to people in shorter bursts, through
feeds, alerts, clips, and links, rather than in one fixed package. In a
way, news is becoming less like a newspaper on the doorstep and
more like a stream that follows you around.
[原因其實很直接：重心本身已經開始轉移。現有研究指出，人們對傳統媒體的投入正在下
降，而對社交媒體、影片平台、搜尋，以及其他數碼途徑的依賴則在增加。所以，未來的新聞
很可能會以更短、更碎片化的形式出現，透過動態消息、通知、短片和連結不斷送到人們面
前，而不是像以前那樣被打包成一份固定產品。某程度上，新聞已經愈來愈不像一份送到門
口的報紙，反而更像一條一直跟着你流動的資訊河。]

components.english: For example, if a major event happens, a person might first see a phone alert or a short video about it, then tap into a news app, search for more context, or watch a quick explainer online. So the first contact with the story may be almost instant, while the deeper understanding comes a step later.
components.chinese: 例如，如果發生一件重大事件，一個人可能會先在手機上看到通知，或者先刷到一段短片，然後再點進新聞應用程式、搜尋更多背景資料，或者看一段簡短解說影片。所以，人們和新聞的第一次接觸很可能會幾乎即時發生，而較深入的理解則會在之後一步一步補上。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a major event happens, a person might
first see a phone alert or a short video about it, then tap into a news
app, search for more context, or watch a quick explainer online. So
the first contact with the story may be almost instant, while the
deeper understanding comes a step later.
[例如，如果發生一件重大事件，一個人可能會先在手機上看到通知，或者先刷到一段短片
，然後再點進新聞應用程式、搜尋更多背景資料，或者看一段簡短解說影片。所以，人們和
新聞的第一次接觸很可能會幾乎即時發生，而較深入的理解則會在之後一步一步補上。]

components.english: So, overall, I’d say future news consumption will probably be quicker, more on-demand, and much more woven into people’s everyday scrolling habits.
components.chinese: 所以，整體來說，我會說未來的新聞接收方式很可能會更快、更即時，也更深地嵌進人們每天滑手機的習慣裡。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, overall, I’d say future news consumption will
probably be quicker, more on-demand, and much more woven into
people’s everyday scrolling habits.
[所以，整體來說，我會說未來的新聞接收方式很可能會更快、更即時，也更深地嵌進人們
每天滑手機的習慣裡。]

components.english: That said, I do not think traditional journalism will disappear; I think the real change will be in how people discover news, not necessarily in where they go to verify it.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為傳統新聞工作會消失；我認為真正改變的，會是人們如何「發現」新聞，而不一定是他們最後去甚麼地方核實新聞。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think traditional journalism will
disappear; I think the real change will be in how people discover
news, not necessarily in where they go to verify it.
[不過，我並不認為傳統新聞工作會消失；我認為真正改變的，會是人們如何「發現」新聞，
而不一定是他們最後去甚麼地方核實新聞。]

components.english: Younger adults are already especially likely to come across news on social media, often without actively looking for it. But that does not mean every platform will become equally trusted. My guess is that people will increasingly discover breaking news through social media and video, then turn to established outlets, websites, podcasts, or explainers when they want the full picture. AI summaries may grow as a supporting layer too, but for now they are still a relatively minor route compared with websites, apps, search, and social platforms.
components.chinese: 年輕成年人其實已經特別容易在社交媒體上偶然碰到新聞，很多時候甚至不是主動去找。不過，這並不代表每一種平台都會得到同等程度的信任。我猜未來人們會愈來愈常在社交媒體和影片平台上先發現突發新聞，之後再轉向較成熟的新聞媒體、網站、播客，或者解說內容去看完整畫面。人工智能摘要也許會慢慢成為一層輔助，但至少目前來看，和網站、應用程式、搜尋及社交平台相比，它仍然只是相對次要的途徑。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Younger adults are already especially likely to come
across news on social media, often without actively looking for it. But
that does not mean every platform will become equally trusted. My
guess is that people will increasingly discover breaking news through
social media and video, then turn to established outlets, websites,
podcasts, or explainers when they want the full picture. AI summaries
may grow as a supporting layer too, but for now they are still a
relatively minor route compared with websites, apps, search, and
social platforms.
[年輕成年人其實已經特別容易在社交媒體上偶然碰到新聞，很多時候甚至不是主動去找。
不過，這並不代表每一種平台都會得到同等程度的信任。我猜未來人們會愈來愈常在社交
媒體和影片平台上先發現突發新聞，之後再轉向較成熟的新聞媒體、網站、播客，或者解說
內容去看完整畫面。人工智能摘要也許會慢慢成為一層輔助，但至少目前來看，和網站、應
用程式、搜尋及社交平台相比，它仍然只是相對次要的途徑。]

components.english: For instance, someone might first hear about an election result or a natural disaster from a clip on social media, but then read a proper article or listen to a podcast to understand what actually happened and why it matters. In that sense, the headline may arrive in a flash, but the meaning still takes a little longer to unfold.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人可能會先透過社交媒體上的短片知道某次選舉結果，或者某場天災發生了，但之後他仍然會去看一篇完整新聞，或者聽一集播客，才真正明白到底發生了甚麼，以及那件事為甚麼重要。從這個角度來說，標題可能是一瞬間就來到眼前，但它真正的意義仍然需要多一點時間才能展開。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, someone might first hear about an election
result or a natural disaster from a clip on social media, but then read a
proper article or listen to a podcast to understand what actually
happened and why it matters. In that sense, the headline may arrive in
a flash, but the meaning still takes a little longer to unfold.
[例如，一個人可能會先透過社交媒體上的短片知道某次選舉結果，或者某場天災發生了，
但之後他仍然會去看一篇完整新聞，或者聽一集播客，才真正明白到底發生了甚麼，以及那
件事為甚麼重要。從這個角度來說，標題可能是一瞬間就來到眼前，但它真正的意義仍然需
要多一點時間才能展開。]

components.english: Overall, I think people will get their news in the future through a blend of social media, search, apps, video, and perhaps some AI-assisted summaries. The front door will probably be faster and noisier than before, but good reporting will still matter, because sooner or later people need more than a flash of information — they need something they can trust.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為未來人們會透過社交媒體、搜尋、應用程式、影片，甚至某些由人工智能協助整理的摘要來獲取新聞。進入新聞世界的那道門大概會比以前更快，也更嘈雜；但優質新聞報導仍然重要，因為說到底，人們終究需要的不只是一下閃過的資訊，而是一些真正值得信任的內容。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think people will get their news in the future
through a blend of social media, search, apps, video, and perhaps
some AI-assisted summaries. The front door will probably be faster
and noisier than before, but good reporting will still matter, because
sooner or later people need more than a flash of information — they
need something they can trust.
[總結來說，我認為未來人們會透過社交媒體、搜尋、應用程式、影片，甚至某些由人工智能
協助整理的摘要來獲取新聞。進入新聞世界的那道門大概會比以前更快，也更嘈雜；但優質
新聞報導仍然重要，因為說到底，人們終究需要的不只是一下閃過的資訊，而是一些真正值
得信任的內容。]

question.english: How does modern technology affect the delivery of news?
question.chinese: 現代科技如何影響新聞的傳遞方式？

source_lines.text: Topic: How does modern technology affect the delivery of news?
現代科技如何影響新聞的傳遞方式？

components.english: I think modern technology has changed the delivery of news dramatically, mainly by making it much faster, easier to access, and more immediate.
components.chinese: 我認為，現代科技已經大大改變了新聞的傳遞方式，主要是令它變得更快、更容易接觸，也更即時。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think modern technology has changed the delivery of news
dramatically, mainly by making it much faster, easier to access, and
more immediate.
[我認為，現代科技已經大大改變了新聞的傳遞方式，主要是令它變得更快、更容易接觸，
也更即時。]

components.english: In the past, people often had to wait for the newspaper, the evening news, or a scheduled radio broadcast. Now, news can reach people almost instantly through phones, apps, websites, and social media. In that sense, news no longer travels at yesterday’s pace; it moves at the speed of a tap. That can be very useful, especially when something important is happening and people need information straight away.
components.chinese: 以前，人們通常要等報紙、晚間新聞，或者固定時間的電台廣播，才知道發生了甚麼事。現在，新聞幾乎可以透過手機、應用程式、網站，以及社交媒體即時送到人們眼前。從這個角度來看，新聞已經不再以昨天那種節奏前進；它幾乎是以按一下的速度在流動。這其實很有用，尤其當一些重要事情正在發生，而人們需要立刻知道資訊的時候。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: In the past, people often had to wait for the
newspaper, the evening news, or a scheduled radio broadcast. Now,
news can reach people almost instantly through phones, apps,
websites, and social media. In that sense, news no longer travels at
yesterday’s pace; it moves at the speed of a tap. That can be very
useful, especially when something important is happening and people
need information straight away.
[以前，人們通常要等報紙、晚間新聞，或者固定時間的電台廣播，才知道發生了甚麼事。現
在，新聞幾乎可以透過手機、應用程式、網站，以及社交媒體即時送到人們眼前。從這個角
度來看，新聞已經不再以昨天那種節奏前進；它幾乎是以按一下的速度在流動。這其實很有
用，尤其當一些重要事情正在發生，而人們需要立刻知道資訊的時候。]

components.english: For example, if there is a typhoon warning, a transport problem, or a major international event, people can usually find out within minutes just by checking their phone. They do not need to sit around waiting for the next bulletin anymore.
components.chinese: 例如，如果有颱風警告、交通問題，或者一些重大的國際事件，人們通常只要看看手機，幾分鐘內就能知道發生了甚麼。他們已經不需要像以前那樣坐著等下一節新聞報告。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if there is a typhoon warning, a transport
problem, or a major international event, people can usually find out
within minutes just by checking their phone. They do not need to sit
around waiting for the next bulletin anymore.
[例如，如果有颱風警告、交通問題，或者一些重大的國際事件，人們通常只要看看手機，幾
分鐘內就能知道發生了甚麼。他們已經不需要像以前那樣坐著等下一節新聞報告。]

components.english: So, on the positive side, modern technology has made news delivery far quicker and far more convenient than before.
components.chinese: 所以，從正面來看，現代科技的確令新聞傳遞比以前快得多，也方便得多。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, on the positive side, modern technology has made
news delivery far quicker and far more convenient than before.
[所以，從正面來看，現代科技的確令新聞傳遞比以前快得多，也方便得多。]

components.english: That said, I also think technology has made the delivery of news messier in some ways, because speed does not always go hand in hand with accuracy.
components.chinese: 不過，我也覺得科技在某些方面令新聞傳遞變得更混亂，因為速度並不一定總是和準確並肩同行。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I also think technology has made the delivery of
news messier in some ways, because speed does not always go hand
in hand with accuracy.
[不過，我也覺得科技在某些方面令新聞傳遞變得更混亂，因為速度並不一定總是和準確並
肩同行。]

components.english: These days, everyone wants to be first, and when that happens, accuracy can sometimes take a back seat. False information, misleading headlines, and half-checked stories can spread like wildfire before people have time to slow down and think. On top of that, there is simply too much information coming at people all the time, so it becomes harder to separate the signal from the noise. In other words, technology has opened the floodgates, but not everything pouring through is trustworthy.
components.chinese: 現在，大家都想搶先一步，而一旦變成這樣，準確性有時就會被放到次要位置。錯誤資訊、誤導性的標題，以及一些只查證了一半的新聞，都可能在大家還來不及停下來想一想之前，就像野火一樣迅速蔓延。除此之外，資訊量本身也實在太多了，不停朝人們湧過來，所以要分清甚麼是真正重要的訊號、甚麼只是雜音，就變得更困難了。換句話說，科技的確打開了資訊的閘門，但湧進來的東西並不是每一樣都值得信任。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: These days, everyone wants to be first, and when that
happens, accuracy can sometimes take a back seat. False
information, misleading headlines, and half-checked stories can
spread like wildfire before people have time to slow down and think.
On top of that, there is simply too much information coming at people
all the time, so it becomes harder to separate the signal from the
noise. In other words, technology has opened the floodgates, but not
everything pouring through is trustworthy.
[現在，大家都想搶先一步，而一旦變成這樣，準確性有時就會被放到次要位置。錯誤資訊、
誤導性的標題，以及一些只查證了一半的新聞，都可能在大家還來不及停下來想一想之前，
就像野火一樣迅速蔓延。除此之外，資訊量本身也實在太多了，不停朝人們湧過來，所以要
分清甚麼是真正重要的訊號、甚麼只是雜音，就變得更困難了。換句話說，科技的確打開了
資訊的閘門，但湧進來的東西並不是每一樣都值得信任。]

components.english: For instance, a short video clip or a dramatic post on social media can go viral in no time, even if the full story is more complicated or the facts are not completely right. By the time the truth catches up, the rumour may already have done the rounds.
components.chinese: 例如，一段很短的影片，或者一則很吸引眼球的社交媒體貼文，可能很快就會瘋傳，即使事情的全貌其實更複雜，或者當中的事實根本還不完全正確。等到真相慢慢追上來的時候，那些傳言往往早就已經傳了一大圈。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a short video clip or a dramatic post on
social media can go viral in no time, even if the full story is more
complicated or the facts are not completely right. By the time the truth
catches up, the rumour may already have done the rounds.
[例如，一段很短的影片，或者一則很吸引眼球的社交媒體貼文，可能很快就會瘋傳，即使
事情的全貌其實更複雜，或者當中的事實根本還不完全正確。等到真相慢慢追上來的時候，
那些傳言往往早就已經傳了一大圈。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say modern technology has made news delivery both better and more problematic. It has put the world at our fingertips, which is powerful, but it has also made it easier for confusion to travel just as fast as facts.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說現代科技令新聞傳遞同時變得更好，也變得更有問題。它的確把整個世界放到了我們的指尖之上，這是很強大的；但它同時也令混亂和事實一樣，可以同樣快地傳播開去。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say modern technology has made news
delivery both better and more problematic. It has put the world at our
fingertips, which is powerful, but it has also made it easier for
confusion to travel just as fast as facts.
[總結來說，我會說現代科技令新聞傳遞同時變得更好，也變得更有問題。它的確把整個世
界放到了我們的指尖之上，這是很強大的；但它同時也令混亂和事實一樣，可以同樣快地傳
播開去。]

question.english: Do you believe everything you read in the newspaper?
question.chinese: 你會相信報紙上讀到的每一件事嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you believe everything you read in the newspaper?
你會相信報紙上讀到的每一件事嗎？

components.english: No, not everything. I think newspapers can be useful and informative, but I would not take every single word at face value.
components.chinese: 不會，並不是每一件事我都會全盤相信。我認為報紙可以很有用，也能提供資訊，但我不會對每一句話都照單全收。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: No, not everything. I think newspapers can be useful and
informative, but I would not take every single word at face value.
[不會，並不是每一件事我都會全盤相信。我認為報紙可以很有用，也能提供資訊，但我不
會對每一句話都照單全收。]

components.english: The main reason is that newspapers are written by human beings, and human beings are never completely neutral. Even when the basic facts are correct, the way a story is framed can still reflect a certain angle, priority, or bias. In other words, news does not just tell people what happened; it also shapes how people see what happened. That is why I think it is wiser to read carefully and keep a critical mind rather than swallow everything whole.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，報紙始終是人寫的，而人本來就不可能完全中立。即使基本事實是正確的，報道的呈現方式仍然可能反映某種立場、側重點，或者偏向。換句話說，新聞不只是告訴人事情發生了甚麼；它同時也在影響人們怎樣看待那件事。正因如此，我覺得更明智的做法，是仔細去讀並保持批判思考，而不是把所有內容一口吞下。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that newspapers are written by
human beings, and human beings are never completely neutral. Even
when the basic facts are correct, the way a story is framed can still
reflect a certain angle, priority, or bias. In other words, news does not
just tell people what happened; it also shapes how people see what
happened. That is why I think it is wiser to read carefully and keep a
critical mind rather than swallow everything whole.
[主要原因是，報紙始終是人寫的，而人本來就不可能完全中立。即使基本事實是正確的，
報道的呈現方式仍然可能反映某種立場、側重點，或者偏向。換句話說，新聞不只是告訴人
事情發生了甚麼；它同時也在影響人們怎樣看待那件事。正因如此，我覺得更明智的做法，
是仔細去讀並保持批判思考，而不是把所有內容一口吞下。]

components.english: For example, two newspapers may report the same political event or social issue, but one may emphasise the conflict while the other focuses more on the background or the consequences. If I only read one version, I might come away with quite a narrow picture.
components.chinese: 例如，兩份報紙可能都在報道同一場政治事件或社會議題，但其中一份可能會特別強調衝突，另一份則可能更著重背景或後果。如果我只看其中一個版本，我最後得到的圖像就可能相當片面。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, two newspapers may report the same
political event or social issue, but one may emphasise the conflict
while the other focuses more on the background or the
consequences. If I only read one version, I might come away with
quite a narrow picture.
[例如，兩份報紙可能都在報道同一場政治事件或社會議題，但其中一份可能會特別強調衝
突，另一份則可能更著重背景或後果。如果我只看其中一個版本，我最後得到的圖像就可能
相當片面。]

components.english: So no, I would not say I believe everything I read in the newspaper, because even reliable reporting still needs to be read with judgment.
components.chinese: 所以，不會，我不會說自己相信報紙上的每一件事，因為即使是相對可靠的報道，也仍然需要帶著判斷去閱讀。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So no, I would not say I believe everything I read in the
newspaper, because even reliable reporting still needs to be read with
judgment.
[所以，不會，我不會說自己相信報紙上的每一件事，因為即使是相對可靠的報道，也仍然
需要帶著判斷去閱讀。]

components.english: That said, I still think newspapers are generally more trustworthy than a lot of random information online.
components.chinese: 不過，我仍然認為，報紙整體上通常比網上很多雜亂資訊更值得信任。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I still think newspapers are generally more
trustworthy than a lot of random information online.
[不過，我仍然認為，報紙整體上通常比網上很多雜亂資訊更值得信任。]

components.english: To be honest, newspapers usually go through editing, fact-checking, and some level of accountability, whereas online rumours can spread like wildfire without much scrutiny at all. So while I would not treat newspapers as the final word, I would still see them as a more solid starting point. In that sense, the goal is not blind trust, but informed trust.
components.chinese: 老實說，報紙通常會經過編輯、查證，以及某程度上的問責；相反，網上的流言卻往往在幾乎沒有審視的情況下就火速傳開。所以，雖然我不會把報紙當成最終權威，但我仍然會把它看成一個比較扎實的起點。從這個角度來看，重點不是盲目相信，而是有根據地去相信。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, newspapers usually go through
editing, fact-checking, and some level of accountability, whereas
online rumours can spread like wildfire without much scrutiny at all.
So while I would not treat newspapers as the final word, I would still
see them as a more solid starting point. In that sense, the goal is not
blind trust, but informed trust.
[老實說，報紙通常會經過編輯、查證，以及某程度上的問責；相反，網上的流言卻往往在幾
乎沒有審視的情況下就火速傳開。所以，雖然我不會把報紙當成最終權威，但我仍然會把它
看成一個比較扎實的起點。從這個角度來看，重點不是盲目相信，而是有根據地去相信。]

components.english: For instance, if I see a surprising story on social media, I am much more likely to check whether a newspaper or another established news source has reported it before I believe it. That extra step often helps separate facts from noise.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我在社交媒體上看到一則很令人震驚的消息，我通常會先看看報紙或其他較有公信力的新聞來源有沒有報道，之後才決定是否相信。多走這一步，很多時候就能幫助人把事實和雜音分開。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if I see a surprising story on social media, I
am much more likely to check whether a newspaper or another
established news source has reported it before I believe it. That extra
step often helps separate facts from noise.
[例如，如果我在社交媒體上看到一則很令人震驚的消息，我通常會先看看報紙或其他較有
公信力的新聞來源有沒有報道，之後才決定是否相信。多走這一步，很多時候就能幫助人把
事實和雜音分開。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say I do not believe everything I read in the newspaper, but I do take it seriously. Newspapers are worth consulting, but not worth believing blindly; the best way to read them is with an open mind and a clear head.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我不會相信報紙上的每一件事，但我會認真看待它。報紙值得參考，但不值得盲信；最好的讀法，是既保持開放，也保持清醒。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say I do not believe everything I read in
the newspaper, but I do take it seriously. Newspapers are worth
consulting, but not worth believing blindly; the best way to read them
is with an open mind and a clear head.
[總結來說，我不會相信報紙上的每一件事，但我會認真看待它。報紙值得參考，但不值得盲
信；最好的讀法，是既保持開放，也保持清醒。]

question.english: In your opinion, when do people share good news?
question.chinese: 主題：依你看，人們通常會在甚麼時候分享好消息？

source_lines.text: Topic: In your opinion, when do people share good news?
主題：依你看，人們通常會在甚麼時候分享好消息？

components.english: I think people usually share good news when it feels both real and emotionally fresh, especially right after something happy, meaningful, or hard-earned has happened.
components.chinese: 我認為，人們通常會在好消息既已成真、而且情緒仍然很新鮮的時候分享，特別是當一些開心、有意義，或者來之不易的事情剛剛發生之後。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think people usually share good news when it feels both real
and emotionally fresh, especially right after something happy,
meaningful, or hard-earned has happened.
[我認為，人們通常會在好消息既已成真、而且情緒仍然很新鮮的時候分享，特別是當一些
開心、有意義，或者來之不易的事情剛剛發生之後。]

components.english: The main reason is that good news naturally makes people want to connect. When something nice happens, people often do not want to sit on it in silence; they want to celebrate, be understood, and let the happiness spread a little. In that sense, joy tends to spill over. If the news means a lot to them, it often feels too good to keep under wraps for long.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，好消息本身就很自然地會令人想和別人連結。當一件好事發生時，人很多時都不想安安靜靜地把它藏在心裡；他們會想慶祝、想被理解，也想讓那份快樂稍微擴散出去。從這個角度來說，喜悅往往是會滿出來的。如果那個消息對他們很重要，很多時就會好得令人很難長時間不說出來。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that good news naturally makes
people want to connect. When something nice happens, people often
do not want to sit on it in silence; they want to celebrate, be
understood, and let the happiness spread a little. In that sense, joy
tends to spill over. If the news means a lot to them, it often feels too
good to keep under wraps for long.
[主要原因是，好消息本身就很自然地會令人想和別人連結。當一件好事發生時，人很多時
都不想安安靜靜地把它藏在心裡；他們會想慶祝、想被理解，也想讓那份快樂稍微擴散出
去。從這個角度來說，喜悅往往是會滿出來的。如果那個消息對他們很重要，很多時就會好
得令人很難長時間不說出來。]

components.english: For example, if someone passes an important exam, gets a job offer, wins a competition, or becomes engaged, they will often tell their parents, partner, or close friends almost straight away. In moments like that, sharing the news is part of the happiness itself.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人通過了一場重要考試、拿到工作機會、贏得比賽，或者訂婚了，他通常都會幾乎立刻告訴父母、伴侶，或者親近朋友。在這些時刻，分享消息本身其實就是快樂的一部分。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone passes an important exam, gets
a job offer, wins a competition, or becomes engaged, they will often
tell their parents, partner, or close friends almost straight away. In
moments like that, sharing the news is part of the happiness itself.
[例如，如果一個人通過了一場重要考試、拿到工作機會、贏得比賽，或者訂婚了，他通常都
會幾乎立刻告訴父母、伴侶，或者親近朋友。在這些時刻，分享消息本身其實就是快樂的一
部分。]

components.english: So, in general, I would say people tend to share good news when it feels certain, personal, and too joyful to keep to themselves.
components.chinese: 所以，一般來說，我會說人們通常會在好消息已經確定、對自己很重要，而且快樂得難以獨自收藏的時候，把它分享出去。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in general, I would say people tend to share good
news when it feels certain, personal, and too joyful to keep to
themselves.
[所以，一般來說，我會說人們通常會在好消息已經確定、對自己很重要，而且快樂得難以獨
自收藏的時候，把它分享出去。]

components.english: That said, I do not think people always share good news immediately, because timing also depends on the situation and the audience.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為人們一定會立刻分享好消息，因為時機很多時也取決於情況，以及分享對象是誰。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think people always share good news
immediately, because timing also depends on the situation and the
audience.
[不過，我也不認為人們一定會立刻分享好消息，因為時機很多時也取決於情況，以及分享
對象是誰。]

components.english: To be honest, some people prefer to wait until the news is fully confirmed or until they find the right moment. Sometimes they do not want to speak too soon and jinx it; sometimes they want to tell certain people first, such as family, before posting it more widely. In other words, good news may travel fast, but it still travels in stages.
components.chinese: 老實說，有些人會比較傾向等到消息完全落實，或者等到一個合適的時機才說出來。有時候，他們是不想太早講，免得事情有變；有時候，他們則是想先告訴某些重要的人，例如家人，之後才向更多人公開。換句話說，好消息也許傳得很快，但很多時仍然會一層一層地傳開。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, some people prefer to wait until the
news is fully confirmed or until they find the right moment.
Sometimes they do not want to speak too soon and jinx it; sometimes
they want to tell certain people first, such as family, before posting it
more widely. In other words, good news may travel fast, but it still
travels in stages.
[老實說，有些人會比較傾向等到消息完全落實，或者等到一個合適的時機才說出來。有時
候，他們是不想太早講，免得事情有變；有時候，他們則是想先告訴某些重要的人，例如家
人，之後才向更多人公開。換句話說，好消息也許傳得很快，但很多時仍然會一層一層地傳

components.english: For instance, if someone gets promoted or is expecting a baby, they may first tell only their closest family members and wait a while before sharing it with colleagues or on social media. That does not mean they are less happy; it just means they want the timing to feel right.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人升職了，或者正準備迎接孩子出生，他可能會先只告訴最親近的家人，隔一段時間之後才告訴同事，或者放到社交媒體上。這並不代表他沒那麼開心，只是代表他希望時機拿捏得更合適。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone gets promoted or is expecting a
baby, they may first tell only their closest family members and wait a
while before sharing it with colleagues or on social media. That does
not mean they are less happy; it just means they want the timing to
feel right.
[例如，如果一個人升職了，或者正準備迎接孩子出生，他可能會先只告訴最親近的家人，隔
一段時間之後才告訴同事，或者放到社交媒體上。這並不代表他沒那麼開心，只是代表他希
望時機拿捏得更合適。]

components.english: Overall, I think people share good news when it is meaningful, confirmed, and emotionally ready to be shared. In many cases, they do not simply blurt it out the moment they feel happy; they wait until they have truly taken it in themselves and feel that both the timing and the audience are right. Joy likes company, but it also likes timing.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為人們通常會在好消息有意義、已經確定，而且情感上也準備好分享的時候，把它說出來。很多時候，他們不是單純因為開心就立刻說，而是會等到自己既感受到那份喜悅，也覺得時機和對象都對了，才會真正開口。快樂的確喜歡有人作伴，但它同時也很講究時機。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think people share good news when it is
meaningful, confirmed, and emotionally ready to be shared. In many
cases, they do not simply blurt it out the moment they feel happy;
they wait until they have truly taken it in themselves and feel that both
the timing and the audience are right. Joy likes company, but it also
likes timing.
[總結來說，我認為人們通常會在好消息有意義、已經確定，而且情感上也準備好分享的時
候，把它說出來。很多時候，他們不是單純因為開心就立刻說，而是會等到自己既感受到那
份喜悅，也覺得時機和對象都對了，才會真正開口。快樂的確喜歡有人作伴，但它同時也很
講究時機。]

question.english: How do people share good news?
question.chinese: 人們通常怎樣分享好消息？

source_lines.text: Topic: How do people share good news?
人們通常怎樣分享好消息？

components.english: I think people usually share good news in quick and cheerful ways, such as through phone calls, text messages, social media, or face-to-face conversations.
components.chinese: 我認為，人們通常會用一些快速而開心的方式去分享好消息，例如打電話、發訊息、在社交媒體上分享，或者直接面對面告訴別人。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think people usually share good news in quick and cheerful
ways, such as through phone calls, text messages, social media, or
face-to-face conversations.
[我認為，人們通常會用一些快速而開心的方式去分享好消息，例如打電話、發訊息、在社
交媒體上分享，或者直接面對面告訴別人。]

components.english: The main reason is that good news naturally makes people want to reach out. When something positive happens, many people do not want to keep it to themselves; they want to tell the people who matter to them and let the happiness ripple outward. These days, the fastest way is often through messaging apps or social media, because people can share the moment almost instantly. In other words, good news often travels with a spring in its step.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，好消息本身就很自然會令人想和別人分享。當有好事發生時，很多人都不想自己一個人收起來，而是會想立刻告訴那些對自己重要的人，讓那份快樂慢慢向外擴散。今時今日，最快的方法很多時候就是透過通訊軟件或社交媒體，因為人們幾乎可以在事情發生的那一刻就分享出去。換句話說，好消息很多時候都是帶著一股輕快的步伐傳出去的。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that good news naturally makes
people want to reach out. When something positive happens, many
people do not want to keep it to themselves; they want to tell the
people who matter to them and let the happiness ripple outward.
These days, the fastest way is often through messaging apps or
social media, because people can share the moment almost instantly.
In other words, good news often travels with a spring in its step.
[主要原因是，好消息本身就很自然會令人想和別人分享。當有好事發生時，很多人都不想
自己一個人收起來，而是會想立刻告訴那些對自己重要的人，讓那份快樂慢慢向外擴散。今
時今日，最快的方法很多時候就是透過通訊軟件或社交媒體，因為人們幾乎可以在事情發
生的那一刻就分享出去。換句話說，好消息很多時候都是帶著一股輕快的步伐傳出去的。]

components.english: For example, if someone gets a job offer, passes an exam, or wins a prize, they may immediately send a message to their family group chat, call a close friend, or post a photo online. The good news can spread in a matter of minutes.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人拿到工作、考試合格，或者贏了獎，他很可能會立刻在家庭群組裡發訊息、打電話給好朋友，或者在網上發照片。那個好消息很多時幾分鐘之內就可以傳開。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone gets a job offer, passes an
exam, or wins a prize, they may immediately send a message to their
family group chat, call a close friend, or post a photo online. The good
news can spread in a matter of minutes.
[例如，如果一個人拿到工作、考試合格，或者贏了獎，他很可能會立刻在家庭群組裡發訊
息、打電話給好朋友，或者在網上發照片。那個好消息很多時幾分鐘之內就可以傳開。]

components.english: So, one common way people share good news is through quick digital communication, because it is easy, immediate, and emotionally satisfying.
components.chinese: 所以，人們分享好消息其中一個很常見的方法，就是透過快速的數碼溝通，因為這樣既方便、即時，也能帶來很直接的情感滿足。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one common way people share good news is
through quick digital communication, because it is easy, immediate,
and emotionally satisfying.
[所以，人們分享好消息其中一個很常見的方法，就是透過快速的數碼溝通，因為這樣既方
便、即時，也能帶來很直接的情感滿足。]

components.english: That said, when the news is especially important or deeply personal, people often prefer to share it in a warmer and more intimate way.
components.chinese: 不過，當那個消息特別重要，或者帶有很深的個人意義時，人們很多時候反而會選擇用一種更溫暖、更親密的方式去分享。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, when the news is especially important or deeply
personal, people often prefer to share it in a warmer and more
intimate way.
[不過，當那個消息特別重要，或者帶有很深的個人意義時，人們很多時候反而會選擇用一
種更溫暖、更親密的方式去分享。]

components.english: Some good news deserves more than a quick text. If the moment is emotionally significant, people often want to see the other person’s reaction, hear the excitement in their voice, or make the memory feel more special. A message may be fast, but a real conversation gives the moment more heart. In that sense, not all good news belongs on a public stage; some of it is better passed hand to hand, heart to heart.
components.chinese: 有些好消息其實值得的，不只是一則很快的訊息而已。如果那個時刻在情感上很有分量，人們很多時候會想親眼看到對方的反應、聽見對方聲音裡的興奮，或者讓那段回憶本身更特別。一則訊息固然很快，但真正的對話卻能讓那個時刻更有溫度。從這個角度來看，不是所有好消息都適合放到公開舞台上；有些消息更適合在人與人之間、心與心之間慢慢傳遞。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Some good news deserves more than a quick text. If
the moment is emotionally significant, people often want to see the
other person’s reaction, hear the excitement in their voice, or make
the memory feel more special. A message may be fast, but a real
conversation gives the moment more heart. In that sense, not all good
news belongs on a public stage; some of it is better passed hand to
hand, heart to heart.
[有些好消息其實值得的，不只是一則很快的訊息而已。如果那個時刻在情感上很有分量，
人們很多時候會想親眼看到對方的反應、聽見對方聲音裡的興奮，或者讓那段回憶本身更
特別。一則訊息固然很快，但真正的對話卻能讓那個時刻更有溫度。從這個角度來看，不是
所有好消息都適合放到公開舞台上；有些消息更適合在人與人之間、心與心之間慢慢傳

components.english: For instance, if someone is getting married, becoming a parent, or recovering from a difficult period in life, they may choose to tell close family members in person over a meal or by making a heartfelt phone call. That way, the news does not just get delivered; it lands properly.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人要結婚、即將做父母，或者終於走過人生一段艱難時期，他可能會選擇在吃飯時親自告訴家人，或者打一通很真摯的電話。這樣一來，那個消息就不只是被送出去，而是真正落到了對方心裡。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone is getting married, becoming a
parent, or recovering from a difficult period in life, they may choose to
tell close family members in person over a meal or by making a
heartfelt phone call. That way, the news does not just get delivered; it
lands properly.
[例如，如果一個人要結婚、即將做父母，或者終於走過人生一段艱難時期，他可能會選擇
在吃飯時親自告訴家人，或者打一通很真摯的電話。這樣一來，那個消息就不只是被送出去
，而是真正落到了對方心裡。]

components.english: Overall, I would say people share good news in different ways depending on the situation. Small happy moments may fly through messages and social media, while bigger and more meaningful ones are often shared more personally, because joy is sweetest when it is truly felt, not just announced.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說人們會按情況用不同方式去分享好消息。較小的快樂時刻也許會透過訊息和社交媒體迅速傳開，而較重大、較有意義的消息，則很多時候會用更個人的方式去分享，因為喜悅最甜的地方，在於它被真正感受到，而不只是被宣佈出來。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I would say people share good news in
different ways depending on the situation. Small happy moments may
fly through messages and social media, while bigger and more
meaningful ones are often shared more personally, because joy is
sweetest when it is truly felt, not just announced.
[總結來說，我會說人們會按情況用不同方式去分享好消息。較小的快樂時刻也許會透過訊
息和社交媒體迅速傳開，而較重大、較有意義的消息，則很多時候會用更個人的方式去分享
，因為喜悅最甜的地方，在於它被真正感受到，而不只是被宣佈出來。]

question.english: What is the difference between new media and old media?
question.chinese: 新媒體和傳統媒體有甚麼分別？

source_lines.text: Topic: What is the difference between new media and old media?
新媒體和傳統媒體有甚麼分別？

components.english: I think the biggest difference is that old media is usually slower and more one-way, whereas new media is faster, more interactive, and far more immediate.
components.chinese: 我認為，兩者最大的分別在於：傳統媒體通常比較慢，而且是單向傳遞的；相反，新媒體更快、更有互動性，也更加即時。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think the biggest difference is that old media is usually
slower and more one-way, whereas new media is faster, more
interactive, and far more immediate.
[我認為，兩者最大的分別在於：傳統媒體通常比較慢，而且是單向傳遞的；相反，新媒體更
快、更有互動性，也更加即時。]

components.english: Old media usually refers to things like newspapers, television, and radio. Those platforms mainly deliver information from the producer to the audience in a fairly fixed format. New media, by contrast, includes social media, websites, apps, streaming platforms, and online video. It moves at the speed of a thumb swipe. People do not just receive content anymore; they react to it, comment on it, share it, remix it, and sometimes create it themselves. In that sense, old media is more like a stage, while new media is more like a crowded town square.
components.chinese: 傳統媒體通常是指報紙、電視和電台這一類平台。這些媒體大多是以一種較固定的形式，把資訊由製作者單向傳給受眾。相比之下，新媒體則包括社交媒體、網站、應用程式、串流平台，以及網上影片。它的速度快得幾乎像手指一滑那樣。現在的人不再只是接收內容而已；他們還會回應、留言、轉發、改編，甚至自己創作。從這個角度來看，傳統媒體比較像一個舞台，而新媒體則更像一個擠滿了人的廣場。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: Old media usually refers to things like newspapers,
television, and radio. Those platforms mainly deliver information from
the producer to the audience in a fairly fixed format. New media, by
contrast, includes social media, websites, apps, streaming platforms,
and online video. It moves at the speed of a thumb swipe. People do
not just receive content anymore; they react to it, comment on it,
share it, remix it, and sometimes create it themselves. In that sense,
old media is more like a stage, while new media is more like a
crowded town square.
[傳統媒體通常是指報紙、電視和電台這一類平台。這些媒體大多是以一種較固定的形式，
把資訊由製作者單向傳給受眾。相比之下，新媒體則包括社交媒體、網站、應用程式、串流
平台，以及網上影片。它的速度快得幾乎像手指一滑那樣。現在的人不再只是接收內容而已
；他們還會回應、留言、轉發、改編，甚至自己創作。從這個角度來看，傳統媒體比較像一個
舞台，而新媒體則更像一個擠滿了人的廣場。]

components.english: For example, if there is breaking news, old media may report it in a scheduled news bulletin or in the next day’s newspaper. New media, however, can spread it within seconds through posts, videos, and live updates, with ordinary users joining the conversation almost immediately.
components.chinese: 例如，如果有突發新聞，傳統媒體可能會在定時新聞報道中播出，或者等到第二天的報紙才刊登出來。不過，新媒體卻可以在幾秒鐘之內，透過帖文、影片和即時更新把消息傳開，而普通用戶也幾乎可以立刻加入討論。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if there is breaking news, old media may
report it in a scheduled news bulletin or in the next day’s newspaper.
New media, however, can spread it within seconds through posts,
videos, and live updates, with ordinary users joining the conversation
almost immediately.
[例如，如果有突發新聞，傳統媒體可能會在定時新聞報道中播出，或者等到第二天的報紙
才刊登出來。不過，新媒體卻可以在幾秒鐘之內，透過帖文、影片和即時更新把消息傳開，
而普通用戶也幾乎可以立刻加入討論。]

components.english: So, one major difference is that new media is much quicker and more participatory, while old media is generally more fixed and one-directional.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個很大的分別就是：新媒體更快，也更講求參與；而傳統媒體通常比較固定，而且是由上而下地單向傳播。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major difference is that new media is much
quicker and more participatory, while old media is generally more
fixed and one-directional.
[所以，其中一個很大的分別就是：新媒體更快，也更講求參與；而傳統媒體通常比較固定，
而且是由上而下地單向傳播。]

components.english: Another important difference is that old media is often seen as more filtered and authoritative, while new media is more open but also more chaotic.
components.chinese: 另一個重要分別是，傳統媒體很多時候被視為較經過篩選、也較有權威；而新媒體則較開放，但同時也更混亂。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: Another important difference is that old media is often seen
as more filtered and authoritative, while new media is more open but
also more chaotic.
[另一個重要分別是，傳統媒體很多時候被視為較經過篩選、也較有權威；而新媒體則較開
放，但同時也更混亂。]

components.english: In old media, content usually goes through editors, producers, or other gatekeepers before it reaches the public. That can make it slower, but it can also make it more structured and, in many cases, more reliable. New media throws the gate wide open. That is one of its greatest strengths, because more voices can be heard. However, it also means rumours, half-truths, and low-quality content can spread like wildfire. In other words, old media may be more controlled, but new media is more democratic — and democracy can be noisy.
components.chinese: 在傳統媒體裡，內容通常要先經過編輯、製作人，或者其他把關者處理，才會到達公眾眼前。這樣做固然會令速度慢一些，但同時也會令內容更有結構，而且在很多情況下也更可靠。至於新媒體，則幾乎是把大門整個打開。這是它最強大的優點之一，因為更多聲音終於有機會被聽見。不過，這同時也意味著流言、半真半假的資訊，以及低質內容，也可以像野火一樣迅速蔓延。換句話說，傳統媒體也許更受控制，但新媒體更民主——而民主很多時候本來就不會太安靜。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: In old media, content usually goes through editors,
producers, or other gatekeepers before it reaches the public. That can
make it slower, but it can also make it more structured and, in many
cases, more reliable. New media throws the gate wide open. That is
one of its greatest strengths, because more voices can be heard.
However, it also means rumours, half-truths, and low-quality content
can spread like wildfire. In other words, old media may be more
controlled, but new media is more democratic — and democracy can
be noisy.
[在傳統媒體裡，內容通常要先經過編輯、製作人，或者其他把關者處理，才會到達公眾眼
前。這樣做固然會令速度慢一些，但同時也會令內容更有結構，而且在很多情況下也更可
靠。至於新媒體，則幾乎是把大門整個打開。這是它最強大的優點之一，因為更多聲音終於
有機會被聽見。不過，這同時也意味著流言、半真半假的資訊，以及低質內容，也可以像野
火一樣迅速蔓延。換句話說，傳統媒體也許更受控制，但新媒體更民主——而民主很多時候
本來就不會太安靜。]

components.english: For instance, a newspaper article may take longer to publish, but it often gives more context and clearer structure. A viral post online, on the other hand, may grab attention instantly, but sometimes it is all smoke and no fire. It looks dramatic, but the substance may be thin.
components.chinese: 例如，一篇報章文章也許要花較長時間才能刊出，但它往往會提供更多背景，也有更清楚的結構。相反，一則在網上爆紅的帖文，可能會立刻抓住大眾眼球，但有時卻只是虛張聲勢。它看起來很戲劇化，但內容本身未必很紮實。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a newspaper article may take longer to
publish, but it often gives more context and clearer structure. A viral
post online, on the other hand, may grab attention instantly, but
sometimes it is all smoke and no fire. It looks dramatic, but the
substance may be thin.
[例如，一篇報章文章也許要花較長時間才能刊出，但它往往會提供更多背景，也有更清楚
的結構。相反，一則在網上爆紅的帖文，可能會立刻抓住大眾眼球，但有時卻只是虛張聲
勢。它看起來很戲劇化，但內容本身未必很紮實。]

components.english: Overall, I would say old media and new media differ mainly in speed, interaction, and control. Old media is usually steadier and more polished, while new media is faster, louder, and more flexible. Neither is perfect, but together they show how communication has moved from a one-way broadcast to a two-way, and sometimes many-way, conversation.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說傳統媒體和新媒體主要在速度、互動性，以及控制程度上有所不同。傳統媒體通常較穩定、也較精緻；而新媒體則更快、更嘈雜，也更靈活。兩者都不是完美的，但它們放在一起，正好反映了傳播方式如何由過去的單向廣播，走向今天的雙向，甚至多向對話。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I would say old media and new media differ
mainly in speed, interaction, and control. Old media is usually
steadier and more polished, while new media is faster, louder, and
more flexible. Neither is perfect, but together they show how

question.english: Do you think the contents in the newspapers are reliable?
question.chinese: 你認為報紙裡的內容可靠嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think the contents in the newspapers are reliable?
你認為報紙裡的內容可靠嗎？

components.english: I’d say they can be fairly reliable, especially if they come from established newspapers, but I would not treat them as unquestionable truth.
components.chinese: 我會說，它們可以算是相當可靠，尤其如果來自較有公信力的報章，不過我也不會把它們當成完全不容質疑的真理。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I’d say they can be fairly reliable, especially if they come from
established newspapers, but I would not treat them as unquestionable
truth.
[我會說，它們可以算是相當可靠，尤其如果來自較有公信力的報章，不過我也不會把它們
當成完全不容質疑的真理。]

components.english: The main reason is that proper newspapers usually have editors, reporting standards, and some level of fact-checking. They also have a reputation to protect, so they cannot just print anything and hope nobody notices. In that sense, newspapers are generally more trustworthy than random posts online or pure word of mouth. They may not get everything perfect, but they are not usually working in the dark either.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，正規報章通常會有編輯、採訪標準，以及某程度上的事實核實。它們同時也要保護自己的聲譽，所以不能隨便亂寫，然後寄望沒人發現。從這個角度來看，報紙一般的確比網上的隨意帖文，或者道聽途說，更值得信任。它們未必每次都完全正確，但通常也不是在黑暗中亂摸。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that proper newspapers usually
have editors, reporting standards, and some level of fact-checking.
They also have a reputation to protect, so they cannot just print
anything and hope nobody notices. In that sense, newspapers are
generally more trustworthy than random posts online or pure word of
mouth. They may not get everything perfect, but they are not usually
working in the dark either.
[主要原因是，正規報章通常會有編輯、採訪標準，以及某程度上的事實核實。它們同時也
要保護自己的聲譽，所以不能隨便亂寫，然後寄望沒人發現。從這個角度來看，報紙一般的
確比網上的隨意帖文，或者道聽途說，更值得信任。它們未必每次都完全正確，但通常也不
是在黑暗中亂摸。]

components.english: For example, if I wanted to know about an important event, such as a policy change or a major accident, I would usually trust a reputable newspaper more than a social media rumour. At the very least, a newspaper is more likely to give names, quotes, and a clearer account of what happened.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我想知道一件重要事情，例如政策改動或重大事故，我通常會比較相信有公信力的報紙，而不是社交媒體上的流言。至少，報紙較有可能提供名字、引述，以及對事情較清楚的交代。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if I wanted to know about an important
event, such as a policy change or a major accident, I would usually
trust a reputable newspaper more than a social media rumour. At the
very least, a newspaper is more likely to give names, quotes, and a
clearer account of what happened.
[例如，如果我想知道一件重要事情，例如政策改動或重大事故，我通常會比較相信有公信
力的報紙，而不是社交媒體上的流言。至少，報紙較有可能提供名字、引述，以及對事情較
清楚的交代。]

components.english: So yes, I do think newspapers can be reliable, and in many cases they are still a much stronger starting point than gossip or online noise.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我的確認為報紙可以是可靠的，而且在很多情況下，它們仍然是比流言或網上雜音更穩妥的起點。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I do think newspapers can be reliable, and in
many cases they are still a much stronger starting point than gossip
or online noise.
[所以，是的，我的確認為報紙可以是可靠的，而且在很多情況下，它們仍然是比流言或網
上雜音更穩妥的起點。]

components.english: That said, reliable does not always mean neutral, and that is where I think people need to be careful.
components.chinese: 不過，可靠並不一定等於中立，而我認為這正是人們需要小心的地方。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, reliable does not always mean neutral, and that is
where I think people need to be careful.
[不過，可靠並不一定等於中立，而我認為這正是人們需要小心的地方。]

components.english: Newspapers can still be biased, selective, or a little sensational at times. Two papers may report the same event, yet frame it in completely different ways depending on their stance or priorities. So even if the facts are broadly correct, the angle may still tilt one way or another. In other words, a newspaper can tell the truth and still not tell the whole truth. That is why I think readers should keep their eyes open and not swallow every headline whole.
components.chinese: 報紙有時仍然可能帶有偏向、選擇性，甚至會有一點煽情。兩份報紙也許在報導同一件事，但卻可能因為立場或重點不同，而用完全不一樣的方式去呈現。所以，即使大致事實沒有錯，角度也可能仍然向某一邊傾斜。換句話說，一份報紙可以說真話，但未必把整個真相都說完整。這也就是為甚麼我認為讀者要保持清醒，不要把每一個標題都照單全收。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Newspapers can still be biased, selective, or a little
sensational at times. Two papers may report the same event, yet
frame it in completely different ways depending on their stance or
priorities. So even if the facts are broadly correct, the angle may still
tilt one way or another. In other words, a newspaper can tell the truth
and still not tell the whole truth. That is why I think readers should
keep their eyes open and not swallow every headline whole.
[報紙有時仍然可能帶有偏向、選擇性，甚至會有一點煽情。兩份報紙也許在報導同一件事
，但卻可能因為立場或重點不同，而用完全不一樣的方式去呈現。所以，即使大致事實沒有
錯，角度也可能仍然向某一邊傾斜。換句話說，一份報紙可以說真話，但未必把整個真相都
說完整。這也就是為甚麼我認為讀者要保持清醒，不要把每一個標題都照單全收。]

components.english: For instance, one newspaper might report a government decision by focusing on its economic benefits, while another might focus on the public backlash. Neither report is necessarily false, but each one shines a different light on the same picture.
components.chinese: 例如，一份報紙在報導某項政府決定時，可能會著重它帶來的經濟好處；另一份則可能會集中寫公眾反彈。這兩種報導未必是假的，只不過它們是用不同的光去照同一幅畫面。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, one newspaper might report a
government decision by focusing on its economic benefits, while
another might focus on the public backlash. Neither report is
necessarily false, but each one shines a different light on the same
picture.
[例如，一份報紙在報導某項政府決定時，可能會著重它帶來的經濟好處；另一份則可能會
集中寫公眾反彈。這兩種報導未必是假的，只不過它們是用不同的光去照同一幅畫面。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say newspaper contents are fairly reliable if the paper is reputable, but they should still be read with a thinking mind. I would trust a good newspaper more than a rumour online, but I would never trust it with my brain switched off.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，如果是有公信力的報章，它的內容通常是相當可靠的，但讀的人仍然要帶著思考去看。我會比較相信一份好的報紙，而不是網上的流言，不過我也絕不會在關掉腦袋的情況下去相信它。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say newspaper contents are fairly reliable
if the paper is reputable, but they should still be read with a thinking
mind. I would trust a good newspaper more than a rumour online, but
I would never trust it with my brain switched off.
[總結來說，我會說，如果是有公信力的報章，它的內容通常是相當可靠的，但讀的人仍然
要帶著思考去看。我會比較相信一份好的報紙，而不是網上的流言，不過我也絕不會在關掉
腦袋的情況下去相信它。]

question.english: Do you think it’s necessary for people to watch foreign news?
question.chinese: 主題：你認為人們有必要看外國新聞嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think it’s necessary for people to watch foreign news?
[主題：你認為人們有必要看外國新聞嗎？]

components.english: Yes, to a certain extent, I do think it is necessary for people to follow some foreign news, because the world is far too connected for us to focus only on what happens at home.
components.chinese: 是的，在某程度上，我的確認為人們有必要接觸一些外國新聞，因為這個世界彼此連繫得太緊密了，我們不能只把目光放在自己地方發生的事。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, to a certain extent, I do think it is necessary for people to
follow some foreign news, because the world is far too connected for
us to focus only on what happens at home.
[是的，在某程度上，我的確認為人們有必要接觸一些外國新聞，因為這個世界彼此連繫得
太緊密了，我們不能只把目光放在自己地方發生的事。]

components.english: The main reason is that events in other countries often spill across borders. A war, an economic crisis, a disease outbreak, or even a major election elsewhere can affect prices, travel, jobs, public opinion, and daily life in ways people may not expect. In that sense, foreign news is not really “someone else’s business” anymore. The world is smaller than it looks on the map. If people never look beyond their own doorstep, their understanding of reality can become a little too narrow.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，其他國家發生的事情很多時候都會跨越邊界，影響到我們。戰爭、經濟危機、疾病爆發，甚至外地的重要選舉，都可能以人們意想不到的方式影響物價、旅遊、工作、輿論，以及日常生活。從這個角度來說，外國新聞其實已經不再只是「別人的事」。世界比地圖上看起來要小得多。如果人們從來不把目光放到自己門外，他們對現實的理解就很容易變得太狹窄。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that events in other countries
often spill across borders. A war, an economic crisis, a disease
outbreak, or even a major election elsewhere can affect prices, travel,
jobs, public opinion, and daily life in ways people may not expect. In
that sense, foreign news is not really “someone else’s business”
anymore. The world is smaller than it looks on the map. If people
never look beyond their own doorstep, their understanding of reality
can become a little too narrow.
[主要原因是，其他國家發生的事情很多時候都會跨越邊界，影響到我們。戰爭、經濟危機、
疾病爆發，甚至外地的重要選舉，都可能以人們意想不到的方式影響物價、旅遊、工作、輿
論，以及日常生活。從這個角度來說，外國新聞其實已經不再只是「別人的事」。世界比地圖
上看起來要小得多。如果人們從來不把目光放到自己門外，他們對現實的理解就很容易變
得太狹窄。]

components.english: For example, if there is a major conflict overseas, it may affect fuel prices, supply chains, or the cost of goods in local shops. In the same way, if another country introduces an important new policy or technology, that can also influence business, education, or public debate elsewhere. So foreign news may feel distant at first, but very often it travels closer than people think.
components.chinese: 例如，如果海外出現重大衝突，它可能會影響燃料價格、供應鏈，甚至本地商店裡貨品的價格。同樣地，如果另一個國家推出重要的新政策或新科技，那也可能影響其他地方的商業、教育，或者公共討論。所以，外國新聞乍看之下也許很遙遠，但很多時候，它其實比人們想像中更快走到身邊。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if there is a major conflict overseas, it may
affect fuel prices, supply chains, or the cost of goods in local shops.
In the same way, if another country introduces an important new
policy or technology, that can also influence business, education, or
public debate elsewhere. So foreign news may feel distant at first, but
very often it travels closer than people think.
[例如，如果海外出現重大衝突，它可能會影響燃料價格、供應鏈，甚至本地商店裡貨品的
價格。同樣地，如果另一個國家推出重要的新政策或新科技，那也可能影響其他地方的商
業、教育，或者公共討論。所以，外國新聞乍看之下也許很遙遠，但很多時候，它其實比人們
想像中更快走到身邊。]

components.english: So yes, I would say following some foreign news is important, because it helps people understand the bigger picture rather than living in a bubble.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說接觸一些外國新聞是重要的，因為這能幫助人們看見更大的整體圖畫，而不是活在自己的小泡泡裡。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say following some foreign news is
important, because it helps people understand the bigger picture
rather than living in a bubble.
[所以，是的，我會說接觸一些外國新聞是重要的，因為這能幫助人們看見更大的整體圖畫
，而不是活在自己的小泡泡裡。]

components.english: That said, I do not think people need to follow foreign news obsessively, or try to know every detail about every country.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為人們需要過度沉迷於外國新聞，或者逼自己知道每一個國家、每一件事的所有細節。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think people need to follow foreign news
obsessively, or try to know every detail about every country.
[不過，我並不認為人們需要過度沉迷於外國新聞，或者逼自己知道每一個國家、每一件事
的所有細節。]

components.english: The problem is that the news cycle can be overwhelming. If people try to consume everything, they may end up drowning in headlines rather than understanding anything properly. So, to me, the real issue is not quantity, but balance. It is better to stay informed in a thoughtful way than to scroll endlessly through a storm of updates. A little clear knowledge is often worth more than a mountain of noise.
components.chinese: 問題在於，新聞循環本身可以非常令人吃不消。如果人們甚麼都想吸收，到最後很可能不是變得更明白，反而只是被一大堆標題淹沒。所以對我來說，真正重要的不是數量，而是平衡。與其無止境地在一場資訊風暴裡不停滑動，不如以有思考的方式保持了解。有時候，一點清楚而扎實的知識，比一座雜音堆成的山更有價值。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: The problem is that the news cycle can be
overwhelming. If people try to consume everything, they may end up
drowning in headlines rather than understanding anything properly.
So, to me, the real issue is not quantity, but balance. It is better to stay
informed in a thoughtful way than to scroll endlessly through a storm
of updates. A little clear knowledge is often worth more than a
mountain of noise.
[問題在於，新聞循環本身可以非常令人吃不消。如果人們甚麼都想吸收，到最後很可能不
是變得更明白，反而只是被一大堆標題淹沒。所以對我來說，真正重要的不是數量，而是平
衡。與其無止境地在一場資訊風暴裡不停滑動，不如以有思考的方式保持了解。有時候，一
點清楚而扎實的知識，比一座雜音堆成的山更有價值。]

components.english: For instance, a person does not need to watch international news all day long, but it is still useful to keep an eye on major world events and understand how they might affect their own country. That way, they stay informed without letting the news take over their whole mental space.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人不需要整天看國際新聞，但留意一些重要世界大事，並理解那些事情可能怎樣影響自己所在的地方，仍然是有用的。這樣一來，他既能保持資訊敏感度，又不至於讓新聞完全佔據自己的心理空間。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a person does not need to watch
international news all day long, but it is still useful to keep an eye on
major world events and understand how they might affect their own
country. That way, they stay informed without letting the news take
over their whole mental space.
[例如，一個人不需要整天看國際新聞，但留意一些重要世界大事，並理解那些事情可能怎
樣影響自己所在的地方，仍然是有用的。這樣一來，他既能保持資訊敏感度，又不至於讓新
聞完全佔據自己的心理空間。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say it is necessary for people to watch some foreign news, but in a measured way rather than an excessive one. The point is not to know everything happening everywhere, but to stay awake to the world beyond your own horizon.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說人們有必要看一些外國新聞，但應該是有分寸地看，而不是過量地看。重點不在於知道世界每個角落發生的所有事情，而是在於對自己視野以外的世界保持清醒和敏感。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say it is necessary for people to watch
some foreign news, but in a measured way rather than an excessive
one. The point is not to know everything happening everywhere, but
to stay awake to the world beyond your own horizon.
[總結來說，我會說人們有必要看一些外國新聞，但應該是有分寸地看，而不是過量地
看。重點不在於知道世界每個角落發生的所有事情，而是在於對自己視野以外的世界
保持清醒和敏感。]

question.english: How has social media changed how we consume news?
question.chinese: 社交媒體如何改變了我們接收新聞的方式？

source_lines.text: Topic: How has social media changed how we consume news?
社交媒體如何改變了我們接收新聞的方式？

components.english: I think social media has changed news consumption enormously, mainly because people now come across news much more casually and much more quickly than before.
components.chinese: 我認為，社交媒體已經大大改變了人們接收新聞的方式，主要是因為現在大家接觸新聞時，比以前更隨意，也更快。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think social media has changed news consumption
enormously, mainly because people now come across news much
more casually and much more quickly than before.
[我認為，社交媒體已經大大改變了人們接收新聞的方式，主要是因為現在大家接觸新聞時
，比以前更隨意，也更快。]

components.english: In the past, people often went looking for the news. They bought a newspaper, turned on the TV, or visited a news website on purpose. Now, news often finds them while they are scrolling. It appears in the middle of videos, posts, comments, and entertainment, so the whole experience feels less formal and more like a constant stream. In a way, news has moved from being a destination to being part of the feed.
components.chinese: 以前，人們通常是主動去找新聞的。他們會買報紙、開電視，或者特地去新聞網站看。但現在，新聞往往是在他們滑手機的時候自己跑到眼前。它會出現在影片、貼文、留言和娛樂內容之間，所以整個接收過程變得沒有那麼正式，反而更像一股不停流動的資訊。某程度上，新聞已經從一個人要特地去到達的「目的地」，變成了資訊流裡的一部分。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: In the past, people often went looking for the news.
They bought a newspaper, turned on the TV, or visited a news website
on purpose. Now, news often finds them while they are scrolling. It
appears in the middle of videos, posts, comments, and entertainment,
so the whole experience feels less formal and more like a constant
stream. In a way, news has moved from being a destination to being
part of the feed.
[以前，人們通常是主動去找新聞的。他們會買報紙、開電視，或者特地去新聞網站看。但現
在，新聞往往是在他們滑手機的時候自己跑到眼前。它會出現在影片、貼文、留言和娛樂內
容之間，所以整個接收過程變得沒有那麼正式，反而更像一股不停流動的資訊。某程度上，
新聞已經從一個人要特地去到達的「目的地」，變成了資訊流裡的一部分。]

components.english: For example, someone might open Instagram, Facebook, or TikTok just to relax for a few minutes, but then end up seeing a breaking story, a political clip, or a headline about something major that happened that day. They were not necessarily looking for the news, but they still ran into it.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人本來可能只是打開 Instagram、Facebook，或者 TikTok，想輕鬆看一下，但結果卻會看到一則突發新聞、一段政治片段，或者一條關於當天重大事件的標題。他原本未必是特意去找新聞，但仍然在過程中碰上了它。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, someone might open Instagram, Facebook,
or TikTok just to relax for a few minutes, but then end up seeing a
breaking story, a political clip, or a headline about something major
that happened that day. They were not necessarily looking for the
news, but they still ran into it.
[例如，一個人本來可能只是打開 Instagram、Facebook，或者 TikTok，想輕鬆看一下，但
結果卻會看到一則突發新聞、一段政治片段，或者一條關於當天重大事件的標題。他原本未
必是特意去找新聞，但仍然在過程中碰上了它。]

components.english: So, one major change is that social media has made news feel more immediate, more casual, and much more woven into everyday life.
components.chinese: 所以，一個很明顯的改變就是：社交媒體令新聞變得更即時、更日常，也更深地滲進了每天的生活裡。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major change is that social media has made
news feel more immediate, more casual, and much more woven into
everyday life.
[所以，一個很明顯的改變就是：社交媒體令新聞變得更即時、更日常，也更深地滲進了每
天的生活裡。]

components.english: That said, I also think social media has made news consumption messier, because it blurs the line between reporting, opinion, entertainment, and even misinformation.
components.chinese: 不過，我也覺得社交媒體令接收新聞這件事變得更混亂，因為它模糊了報道、意見、娛樂，甚至錯誤資訊之間的界線。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I also think social media has made news
consumption messier, because it blurs the line between reporting,
opinion, entertainment, and even misinformation.
[不過，我也覺得社交媒體令接收新聞這件事變得更混亂，因為它模糊了報道、意見、娛樂，
甚至錯誤資訊之間的界線。]

components.english: These days, people do not just get news from news organisations. They also get it from creators, influencers, commentators, reposts, and people they do not know personally. That means the news can feel more lively and more accessible, but it can also become harder to judge what is reliable. Very often, the reaction arrives before the reporting, and the headline arrives before the full story.
components.chinese: 現在，人們接收新聞時，來源已經不只是傳統新聞機構了。他們也會從創作者、網紅、評論者、轉發貼文，甚至一些自己根本不認識的人那裡接觸到新聞。這樣做的確會令新聞感覺更有活力，也更容易接觸，但同時也會令人更難判斷甚麼才是可靠的。很多時候，反應會比報道先到，標題也會比完整故事先到。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: These days, people do not just get news from news
organisations. They also get it from creators, influencers,
commentators, reposts, and people they do not know personally. That
means the news can feel more lively and more accessible, but it can
also become harder to judge what is reliable. Very often, the reaction
arrives before the reporting, and the headline arrives before the full
story.
[現在，人們接收新聞時，來源已經不只是傳統新聞機構了。他們也會從創作者、網紅、評論
者、轉發貼文，甚至一些自己根本不認識的人那裡接觸到新聞。這樣做的確會令新聞感覺更
有活力，也更容易接觸，但同時也會令人更難判斷甚麼才是可靠的。很多時候，反應會比報
道先到，標題也會比完整故事先到。]

components.english: For instance, a short clip or a dramatic post can spread very quickly, and people may form an opinion on the spot before they have read a full article or checked where the information came from. By the time the fuller picture appears, the first impression may already have stuck.
components.chinese: 例如，一段很短的影片，或者一則很吸睛的貼文，往往可以傳得非常快，而人們可能在還沒有看完整篇報道、也沒有查清楚資訊來源之前，就已經先形成了自己的看法。等到更完整的內容出來時，最初的印象往往早就已經留在心裡了。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a short clip or a dramatic post can spread
very quickly, and people may form an opinion on the spot before they
have read a full article or checked where the information came from.
By the time the fuller picture appears, the first impression may
already have stuck.
[例如，一段很短的影片，或者一則很吸睛的貼文，往往可以傳得非常快，而人們可能在還
沒有看完整篇報道、也沒有查清楚資訊來源之前，就已經先形成了自己的看法。等到更完整
的內容出來時，最初的印象往往早就已經留在心裡了。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say social media has made news easier to reach but harder to navigate. It puts information in the palm of our hand, which is powerful, but it also means we have to read with sharper eyes and a more careful mind.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說社交媒體令新聞變得更容易接觸，但也更難判斷和消化。它的確把資訊放到了我們手掌之中，這很強大；但同時也代表，我們必須用更銳利的眼光和更小心的頭腦去閱讀它。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say social media has made news easier to
reach but harder to navigate. It puts information in the palm of our
hand, which is powerful, but it also means we have to read with
sharper eyes and a more careful mind.
[總結來說，我會說社交媒體令新聞變得更容易接觸，但也更難判斷和消化。它的確把資訊
放到了我們手掌之中，這很強大；但同時也代表，我們必須用更銳利的眼光和更小心的頭腦
去閱讀它。]

question.english: What kinds of famous people are usually in the news in your country?
question.chinese: 你國家裡，哪一類知名人士通常最常出現在新聞裡？

source_lines.text: What kinds of famous people are usually in the news in your country?
你國家裡，哪一類知名人士通常最常出現在新聞裡？

components.english: In Hong Kong, I think the people who are most often in the news are usually politicians, government officials, and other public figures whose decisions affect everyday life.
components.chinese: 在香港，我認為最常出現在新聞裡的人，通常都是政治人物、政府官員，以及其他那些會影響日常生活的公眾人物。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: In Hong Kong, I think the people who are most often in the
news are usually politicians, government officials, and other public
figures whose decisions affect everyday life.
[在香港，我認為最常出現在新聞裡的人，通常都是政治人物、政府官員，以及其他那些會
影響日常生活的公眾人物。]

components.english: The main reason is that when a person holds power or influence, what they do tends to ripple out into the wider public. If there are changes in housing, education, transport, healthcare, or the economy, those figures naturally end up in the spotlight. In that sense, they are not just in the news because they are famous; they are in the news because they have their hands on the wheel.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，當一個人擁有權力或影響力時，他所做的事情往往會像漣漪一樣向外擴散到整個社會。如果房屋、教育、交通、醫療，或者經濟方面出現變化，這些人物自然就會被推到聚光燈下。從這個角度來看，他們並不只是因為有名才上新聞；更是因為他們手上握著方向盤。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that when a person holds power or
influence, what they do tends to ripple out into the wider public. If
there are changes in housing, education, transport, healthcare, or the
economy, those figures naturally end up in the spotlight. In that
sense, they are not just in the news because they are famous; they
are in the news because they have their hands on the wheel.
[主要原因是，當一個人擁有權力或影響力時，他所做的事情往往會像漣漪一樣向外擴散到
整個社會。如果房屋、教育、交通、醫療，或者經濟方面出現變化，這些人物自然就會被推到
聚光燈下。從這個角度來看，他們並不只是因為有名才上新聞；更是因為他們手上握著方向

components.english: For example, if there is a new policy, a controversy, or a public debate, senior officials and political leaders are usually the first people reporters focus on. The same is true of major business figures when their decisions affect jobs, prices, or investment.
components.chinese: 例如，如果有新政策、爭議事件，或者公共辯論，記者通常最先聚焦的就是高級官員和政治領袖。如果一些大型商界人物的決定會影響就業、物價，或者投資情況，他們也同樣很容易成為新聞焦點。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if there is a new policy, a controversy, or a
public debate, senior officials and political leaders are usually the first
people reporters focus on. The same is true of major business figures
when their decisions affect jobs, prices, or investment.
[例如，如果有新政策、爭議事件，或者公共辯論，記者通常最先聚焦的就是高級官員和政
治領袖。如果一些大型商界人物的決定會影響就業、物價，或者投資情況，他們也同樣很容
易成為新聞焦點。]

components.english: So, one major group that often appears in the news is powerful public figures, because their choices can shape the way society moves.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一大類最常出現在新聞裡的人，就是那些有權力的公眾人物，因為他們的選擇往往會影響整個社會如何前進。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major group that often appears in the news is
powerful public figures, because their choices can shape the way
society moves.
[所以，其中一大類最常出現在新聞裡的人，就是那些有權力的公眾人物，因為他們的選擇
往往會影響整個社會如何前進。]

components.english: That said, celebrities, entertainers, and sometimes athletes are also very common in the news, because public attention is not driven by politics alone.
components.chinese: 不過，名人、娛樂圈人物，以及有時候運動員，也同樣很常上新聞，因為吸引公眾目光的並不只有政治。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, celebrities, entertainers, and sometimes athletes
are also very common in the news, because public attention is not
driven by politics alone.
[不過，名人、娛樂圈人物，以及有時候運動員，也同樣很常上新聞，因為吸引公眾目光的並
不只有政治。]

components.english: To be honest, people do not live on serious news alone. They are also drawn to stories about film stars, singers, influencers, or sports personalities, especially when those people are involved in major performances, competitions, scandals, or personal drama. In other words, some people make headlines because they shape policy, while others make headlines because they capture imagination.
components.chinese: 老實說，人並不是只靠嚴肅新聞過日子的。大家同樣也會被電影明星、歌手、網絡紅人，或者運動人物的故事吸引，特別是當這些人牽涉到大型演出、比賽、醜聞，或者私人風波時。換句話說，有些人之所以成為頭條，是因為他們影響政策；而另一些人之所以成為頭條，則是因為他們抓住了大眾的想像力。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, people do not live on serious news
alone. They are also drawn to stories about film stars, singers,
influencers, or sports personalities, especially when those people are
involved in major performances, competitions, scandals, or personal
drama. In other words, some people make headlines because they
shape policy, while others make headlines because they capture
imagination.
[老實說，人並不是只靠嚴肅新聞過日子的。大家同樣也會被電影明星、歌手、網絡紅人，或
者運動人物的故事吸引，特別是當這些人牽涉到大型演出、比賽、醜聞，或者私人風波時。
換句話說，有些人之所以成為頭條，是因為他們影響政策；而另一些人之所以成為頭條，則
是因為他們抓住了大眾的想像力。]

components.english: For instance, if a well-known actor stars in a major film, a singer holds a big concert, or an athlete wins an important competition, they are likely to be all over the news. By the same token, if a celebrity gets caught up in controversy, that story can spread like wildfire.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一位知名演員參演了一部大片、一位歌手舉行大型演唱會，或者一位運動員贏得重要比賽，他們很可能就會出現在各大新聞裡。同樣地，如果一位名人捲入爭議，那個故事也可能會像野火一樣迅速傳開。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if a well-known actor stars in a major film, a
singer holds a big concert, or an athlete wins an important
competition, they are likely to be all over the news. By the same
token, if a celebrity gets caught up in controversy, that story can
spread like wildfire.
[例如，如果一位知名演員參演了一部大片、一位歌手舉行大型演唱會，或者一位運動員贏
得重要比賽，他們很可能就會出現在各大新聞裡。同樣地，如果一位名人捲入爭議，那個故
事也可能會像野火一樣迅速傳開。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say the news in Hong Kong usually revolves around two broad kinds of famous people: those with real public power, and those with strong public appeal. One group moves society, while the other keeps people watching.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說香港的新聞通常圍繞兩大類知名人物：一類是真正握有公共影響力的人，另一類則是擁有強大公眾吸引力的人。一類人在推動社會運作，另一類人則讓大眾持續把目光放在他們身上。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say the news in Hong Kong usually
revolves around two broad kinds of famous people: those with real
public power, and those with strong public appeal. One group moves
society, while the other keeps people watching.
[總結來說，我會說香港的新聞通常圍繞兩大類知名人物：一類是真正握有公共影響力的人
，另一類則是擁有強大公眾吸引力的人。一類人在推動社會運作，另一類人則讓大眾持續把
目光放在他們身上。]
